title: Washington Post comment section
content:
I'm stumped. For years I kept Firefox on my Windows machine (Win10, 64bit, 8gb ram) at some level around 80x (I forget the number). I even recently switched over to Edge until I got fed up with its complicated design. So when I went back to Firefox, I upgraded to the latest 147.0.3 (64-bit). At first everything seemed okay. But then I noticed I was having trouble posting comments on any Washington Post comment section OR the whole thing would freeze up or other oddities. ONLY there. Everything else runs fine.

To add to the mystery, I walk upstairs to where I have a Mac (Sonoma, with 64gb ram) with Firefox 143.0.1. And I can post comments on the Washington Post, nothing freezes, everything works fine. So it can't be a problem on the Post's site itself.

Back on the Windows machine I have tried various Firefox settings, changing the permissions from strict to standard, changing the performance settings, turning Add ons off and on one by one, running in Safe Mode. But no changes change the problem on the Post comment section.

I'm at a loss to understand the difference. The Mac does have more Ram, but previous to my upgrade on the Windows machine, it was running the Post site just fine.
creator: cricket.jiminy.cricket

title: verification allows all https links open?
content: As many times as I have tried (and failed) to link Google, Gmail, MSFT, Outlook, et al, does this verification now provide official authorized use for Firefox-Mozilla use? Specific to Tor Onion Project. No doubt age,vision, and time frustrations left me was part & parcel to "limits". Far Too Many URL passwords et al verification attributes. i.e. passkey, image i.d., phone, keystroke vs keyboard malfunctions, Android vs PC ALL set the pace for those of us with various disabilities. RVN veterans know the grind. Rare that vets dealing with DOS, Apple II, Windows crashes, C:\mkdir, config.sys, cmd, regedit etc. could/might get up to speed. Especially for folks whose occupations, professional or blue collar could afford the time. One program back then, circa 1980s, Symantec's Q&A4.0. I/we went from 5.25" floppies and a 20MB hd to 3.5", RW disks. The average Joe/Joline aged to the proverbial "FUGGIT ABOUDIT". 32bit become 64bit where old 32 bit Q&A4.0 no longer fit. That's about it for seniors.
